name: Platform-neutral fallback requirements in procurement and AltID integration
consequences: Direct effect: procurement documents and contracts will explicitly require platform-neutral fallback authentication paths and independent trust anchors, reducing single-vendor exposure. Downstream implication: incumbents may resist or demand concessions, potentially extending procurement timelines and adding negotiation complexity. Trade-off: this strengthens sovereignty but could slow adoption and raise early-stage costs.
options: Mandate platform-neutral fallback authentication as a contractual requirement in all AltID procurement documents and pilot programs.
Institute performance-based criteria for fallback paths, including interoperability tests against core EU wallets to demonstrate resilience.
Adopt phased procurement where platform-neutral components are evaluated in non-production pilots first, with explicit go/no-go gates.
review: Core tension: procurement leverage versus incumbent resistance; weakness: options assume regulatory momentum without fully addressing transitional costs or certification bottlenecks that could slow pilots.

name: Non-production demonstrators to credibly prove fallback-capable access
consequences: Direct effect: credible, non-production demonstrations establish evidence that standards-based fallback access is feasible without exclusive reliance on Apple or Google trust anchors. Downstream implication: results may attract regulatory momentum, but may not translate to immediate production readiness; risk of misalignment with MitID live-trust requirements.
options: Develop a mobile demonstrator implementing FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS to show platform-neutral authentication feasibility.
Construct a browser-based fallback pathway using hardware tokens and WebAuthn-compatible flows to demonstrate independent access outside mobile OS trust signals.
Create an end-to-end test suite validating fallback flows against eIDAS2 requirements with an independent verifier.
review: Core tension: demonstration credibility versus production readiness; weakness: non-production scope may underplay live-ops constraints and live MitID dependencies.

name: Policy and regulatory advocacy for platform diversification
consequences: Direct effect: policy groundwork reframes platform dependency as a resilience and continuity risk, pushing procurement neutrality. Downstream implication: political negotiations may stall or be captured by incumbents; potential misalignment with existing vendor contracts. Trade-off: gains in long-term sovereignty may incur short-term friction and slower procurement cycles.
options: Lobby for amendments to public procurement rules that explicitly require platform-neutral identity authentication paths and independent trust anchors.
Propose regulatory guidelines for continuity-of-service planning that mandate fallback authentication options in critical digital services.
Develop a policy toolkit detailing privacy, accountability, and interoperability criteria aligned with eIDAS and NSIS for platform diversification.
review: Core tension: regulatory reform pace vs policy momentum; weakness: options may over-rely on legislative changes that take time, risking delay in tangible actions.

name: EU interoperability alignment and standards push
consequences: Direct effect: strengthens Danish credibility through alignment with EU wallet interoperability standards and decoupled trust anchors. Downstream implication: slower national progress due to EU coordination timelines; potential complexity integrating national standards with EU profiles. Trade-off: enhanced portability and resilience at the cost of potential jurisdictional friction and longer decision cycles.
options: Submit and advocate for Danish alignment with European Digital Identity Wallet interoperability standards, focusing on portable credentials and decoupled trust.
Develop a national standard profile for fallback authentication compatible with EU wallet concepts, including auditable logging and portable keys.
Engage with NSIS technical working groups to incorporate platform-neutral trust anchors into formal evaluation criteria.
review: Core tension: national urgency versus EU alignment timelines; weakness: options may underweight domestic procurement negotiations that can stall while awaiting EU consensus.

name: Coalition-building and resilience framing
consequences: Direct effect: reframes platform dependence as a public-risk issue guiding policy and procurement; strengthens legitimacy for change. Downstream implication: potential pushback from industry and political actors who prefer status quo; risk of fragmentation if coalition cannot maintain a consistent message. Trade-off: broader support may slow consensus formation but improves legitimacy and scrutiny.
options: Coordinate a broad coalition to publish a resilience-centric white paper that reframes platform lock-in as a public risk.
Organize multi-stakeholder seminars and public hearings to surface concerns and gather procurement feedback across branches.
Publish independent risk assessments and transparency reports to compel operator concessions and regulatory scrutiny.
review: Core tension: broad coalition momentum vs fragmented industry interests; weakness: options may not specify concrete decision gates or timelines to translate advocacy into policy.

name: Structured governance and phased demonstration oversight
consequences: Direct effect: improves governance with clear milestones, accountability, and risk controls; downstream implication: added administrative overhead and potential delays if milestones are not met. Trade-off: higher governance rigor reduces risk but increases coordination burden and responsiveness to regulatory cycles.
options: Establish a phased demonstration governance model with clear milestones, gating future budget releases to milestones.
Institute independent security reviews after each milestone to validate compliance with EU frameworks and Danish policy.
Set up a risk-adjusted funding mechanism that reserves contingency for regulatory delays or procurement objections.
review: Core tension: governance rigor vs agility; weakness: options may over-index on process, risking delays if milestones falter and funding is blocked.

name: Procurement language to enforce platform-neutral fallback for identity services
consequences: Requiring platform-neutral fallback in procurement creates a direct constraint on vendor choices, driving attention to open standards and independent trust anchors. A downstream implication is longer tender cycles and potential pushback from incumbents who benefit from existing platform-integrated capabilities. This approach may slow initial procurement but improves long-term continuity if platform policies shift.
options: Embed explicit platform-neutral fallback requirements in all future AltID procurement documents and align conformance criteria with open standards.
Institute a staged procurement path that tests fallback capabilities in parallel with core identity services to reduce single-point risk.
Engage with privacy and civil-society review boards to validate neutrality criteria and publish the evaluation framework publicly.
review: Core tension centers on aligning aggressive platform-neutral mandates with the realities of contract reform and supplier negotiations; the options emphasize language and staged procurement but overlook transitional governance across MitID dependencies.

name: Credible demonstrations of platform-neutral fallback without production risk
consequences: Non-production demonstrations reveal feasibility and build credibility, but procurement authorities may question the relevance of test environments to live MitID operations; if demonstrations omit critical edge cases, implementation risk remains. The approach can rally policy support while avoiding live-system exposure, yet it may be dismissed as theoretical if edge-case scenarios are underrepresented.
options: Develop modular demonstrations using universal open standards (FIDO2/WebAuthn, OpenID Connect) that run on multiple devices including privacy-focused variants.
Coordinate cross-agency showcases that simulate platform outages to illustrate continuity benefits without touching live systems.
Document limitations and gating factors in open technical notes to prevent over-claiming about readiness or scalability.
review: Core tension: credible demonstrations versus live-operations readiness; the options push for credible tech proofs but miss how to ensure edge-case coverage and regulatory relevance in live environments.

name: EU interoperability push within Digital Identity Wallet standards to reduce vendor lock-in
consequences: Aligning with EU standards can unlock cross-border portability and shared resilience, but national procurement timelines may slow as compatibility requirements evolve; risk of mission creep if standards become too broad. The effort can elevate Denmark’s stance on sovereignty while leveraging EU momentum, yet benefits hinge on sustained European engagement and timely standardization progress.
options: Lobby for explicit AltID-related requirements in EU Wallet specifications, including certified fallback pathways and platform-agnostic authentication signals.
Participate in EU working groups to secure language on open interfaces and independent trust anchors, avoiding single-vendor dependence.
Submit national position papers indexing requirements by risk level to prevent over-constraint while preserving procurement flexibility.
review: Core tension: EU harmonization versus national autonomy; the options pursue EU alignment but risk dependency on slower European consensus and funding cycles.

name: Public-privacy resilience framing in procurement and policy
consequences: Reframing as resilience and continuity fosters legitimacy and political support, but may invite criticism if privacy concerns are perceived as secondary to security; risk of over-optimism about fallback viability. The approach helps secure budget and attention, yet must guard against oversimplifying privacy trade-offs and ensuring independent oversight.
options: Develop narrative briefs that quantify resilience benefits and potential continuity costs of platform dependence across essential services.
Convene a coalition of civil-society and academic experts to validate the impact of vendor lock-in on service accessibility during crises.
Incorporate privacy-by-design criteria into fallback designs to address democratic accountability and transparency expectations.
review: Core tension: resilience framing versus privacy safeguards; the options lack concrete privacy measurement and independent auditing plans.

name: Baseline fallback architecture for platform-neutral identity
consequences: Establishing a reference architecture creates a concrete target for vendors and regulators, but may constrain experimentation if too rigid; risk of misalignment with evolving standards. The architecture provides clarity for stakeholders while leaving room for iterative improvements, but could bottleneck adaptation if standards shift rapidly.
options: Draft a reference architecture that specifies open standards, modular components, and hardware-backed trust anchors for fallback authentication.
Define governance and lifecycle management for the fallback anchor, including incident response, updates, and cross-agency access controls.
Map existing MitID critical points to fallback paths to minimize disruption during transition and ensure interoperability.
review: Core tension: fixed architectural blueprint versus evolving identity standards; the options do not specify a process for updating the architecture in response to new platform capabilities.

name: Risk-sharing and contingency financing for resilience initiatives
consequences: A dedicated funding mechanism reduces funding gaps and signals seriousness to stakeholders, but may create bureaucratic overhead or misalignment with annual budget cycles; risk that funds sit idle if milestones slip. The financing approach helps sustain long-term work, yet requires clear governance, oversight, and alignment with evaluation milestones to prevent drift.
options: Propose a multi-year funding plan with milestone-based disbursements tied to demonstrator outcomes and policy commitments.
Establish a cross-agency contingency fund managed by Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to finance pilots, security reviews, and procurement analysis.
Pursue EU structural funds or resilience-focused grants to diversify financing while aligning with European interoperability goals.
review: Core tension: financing certainty versus political and budgetary cycles; the options lack an explicit oversight and audit framework to ensure funds are spent as intended.

name: Diversified trust anchors in procurement for AltID interoperability
consequences: Direct effect: procurement pathways actively favor multiple, standards-based trust anchors beyond a single platform ecosystem, increasing resilience. Downstream: governments gain bargaining power and reduced exposure to platform policy shifts, but this shift can extend procurement cycles and raise certification overhead as new anchors are evaluated. Trade-offs include potential compatibility costs and longer onboarding for agencies.
options: Diversify supplier qualification by requiring open interfaces and certified fallback pathways across AltID components, with clear migration milestones and exit criteria.
Engage in a structured multi-vendor pilot to compare fallback architectures against governance costs and interoperability requirements, with independent review.
Institute cost-sharing arrangements for fallback implementation among agencies, vendors, and academic partners to distribute risk and align incentives.
review: Core tension: diversifying trust anchors challenges incumbent control over MitID dependencies, while timelines pressure fast moves. Weakness: the options may understate political capital and cost required to reframe legacy contracts and vendor relationships.

name: Open standards demonstrator program for platform-neutral fallback
consequences: Direct effect: demonstrates feasibility of standards-based fallback without production integration, reducing risk of vendor lock-in in non-production contexts. Downstream: can attract funding and political support, but may create artifacts that are not production-ready and require careful governance around non-production status.
options: Commission two non-production demonstrators that authenticate against a simulated identity-provider using FIDO2, OpenID Connect, and portable credentials.
Develop a browser-based fallback path using hardware tokens and WebAuthn that minimizes reliance on mobile OS trust signals.
Create a shared evaluation framework to compare vendor-neutral architectures against risk of governance overhead and interoperability constraints.
review: Core tension: proving real-world viability without production deployment challenges, but the options miss a concrete certification pathway and regulatory alignment.

name: Stage-gated resilience governance reform for identity procurement
consequences: Direct effect: introduces governance processes to manage supplier concentration risk and ensure platform-neutral criteria are evaluated; Downstream: increases oversight and potentially slows procurement cycles, requiring clear decision gates and performance metrics.
options: Institute a phased procurement approach with gate reviews at month 8 and 16 to assess platform-neutral progress and adjust scope.
Mandate cross-agency procurement councils to coordinate AltID-related requirements, reduce duplication, and harmonize conflicting standards across agencies.
Adopt a pre-competitive collaboration model with academic and civic-society partners to vet fallback concepts and share risk.
review: Core tension: governance speed versus cross-agency alignment; weakness: gate reviews may become bottlenecks if funding or political will fluctuates.

name: Policy- and EU-aligned platform diversification advocacy
consequences: Direct effect: builds political and regulatory support for platform diversification while signaling resilience and continuity commitments; Downstream: may trigger pushback from incumbents and require sustained lobbying and alignment with EU timelines.
options: Publish a formal policy proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen advocating platform diversification with explicit resilience metrics and benchmarks.
Organize a high-profile regulatory sandbox to test platform-diverse identity channels under NSIS and eIDAS2 constraints.
Coordinate EU-aligned consultation with Digital Identity Wallet stakeholders to align Danish language on fallback requirements.
review: Core tension: policy momentum versus EU compatibility; weakness: reliance on policy windows that can be delayed or deprioritized by competing dossiers.

name: Public financing mechanisms to share fallback-transition risk
consequences: Direct effect: creates funding for transition costs and reduces barrier to certification; Downstream: necessitates governance around fund allocation and milestones, with exposure to budgetary shifts if milestones slip.
options: Establish a contingency fund with matched contributions from government, university researchers, and industry to cover transition costs.
Negotiate milestone-based disbursement tied to demonstrator outcomes to enforce budget discipline and measurable progress milestones.
Create a transparent cost accounting framework to quantify supplier-concentration risk and justify ongoing funding across phases.
review: Core tension: aligning funding flows with long procurement cycles; weakness: contingency funds may be underfunded if milestones slip or political priorities shift.

name: Expanded access channels and user-centric fallback design
consequences: Direct effect: expands access channels away from app stores and OS dependencies; downstream: requires extensive usability and privacy controls, plus regulatory alignment for new channels.
options: Map user journeys to identify alternative authentication channels (browser-based, hardware-token, offline fallback) and prioritize least friction.
Implement privacy-preserving telemetry during demonstrations to quantify risk without exposing production data, and document anonymization methods.
Engage civil-society and privacy advocates to validate accessibility and trust in fallback pathways through transparent workshops.
review: Core tension: usability improvements may widen attack surface and privacy risk; weakness: demonstrations may not fully capture live operational threat vectors or regulatory scrutiny.
